Paragraph

Note that \(\cup_{k=N}^{\infty}D_{k}\) is open and
\begin{equation*} |\cup_{k=N}^{\infty}D_{k}|\le \sum_{k=N}^{\infty}|D_{k}| \le \sum_{k=N}^{\infty}\epsilon_{k}^{p}\to 0\text{,} \end{equation*}
as \(N\to\infty\text{.}\) Therefore, the set \(E\) 1  defined by
\begin{equation*} E=\cap_{N=1}^{\infty} \cup_{k=N}^{\infty}D_{k} \end{equation*}
is negligible, and for any \(x\in E^{c}\text{,}\) there exists some \(N_{x}\) such that \(x\in \cap_{k=N}^{\infty}D_{k}^{c}\text{,}\) which makes \(\{c_{k}(x)\}\) a Cauchy sequence in \(\bbR\text{,}\) therefore \(\lim_{k\to\infty} c_{k}(x)\) exists. Furthermore, for any \(\epsilon > 0\text{,}\) there exists some \(N_{\epsilon}\) such that \(|\cup_{k=N}^{\infty}D_{k}| < \epsilon\text{.}\) On the complement of \(\cup_{k=N}^{\infty}D_{k}\text{,}\) \(\vert c_{k}(x) -c_{k+1}(x)\vert \le \epsilon_{k}\) for all \(k\ge N\text{,}\) implying that \(\{c_{k}(x)\}\) converges uniformly on this set.
in-context
